questions.title: Question 1
questions.question: **Which method in `FeatureEngineeringClient` retrieves feature table metadata?**

choices.B: `describe_table()`
choices.C: `read_table()`
choices.D: `inspect_table()`

questions.shortAnswer: `fe.get_table(name="catalog.schema.feature_table")` retrieves the FeatureTable metadata object including schema, primary keys, and timestamp column.

questions.title: Question 2
questions.question: **A feature table has `user_id` as primary key and `event_time` as the timestamp column. Which `FeatureLookup` parameters are needed for point-in-time retrieval?**

choices.B: `lookup_key="user_id"` and `timestamp_lookup_key="event_time"`
choices.C: `timestamp_lookup_key="event_time"` only
choices.D: `feature_names=["user_id", "event_time"]`

questions.shortAnswer: Both lookup_key (for entity matching) and timestamp_lookup_key (for point-in-time join) are required for temporally correct feature retrieval.

questions.title: Question 3
questions.question: **What is the relationship between the offline and online feature stores?**

choices.A: They are identical — same storage, different access patterns
choices.B: Offline (Delta) stores historical feature values; online (Redis-backed) stores the latest values for real-time serving
choices.C: Online store is a cache of the offline store with 24-hour TTL
choices.D: They are separate systems with no automatic synchronization

questions.shortAnswer: The offline store (Delta Lake) holds all historical feature values for training and batch scoring. The online store holds the latest values for real-time inference. `publish_online_features()` syncs from offline to online.

questions.title: Question 4
questions.question: **A team logs a model using `mlflow.sklearn.log_model()` instead of `fe.log_model()`. What is the consequence for feature lineage?**

choices.A: No consequence — both methods capture feature lineage
choices.B: Feature table dependencies are NOT automatically linked to the model version
choices.C: The model cannot be registered to the UC registry
choices.D: Point-in-time features will not work at serving time

questions.shortAnswer: Only `fe.log_model()` (FeatureEngineeringClient) captures the feature table dependencies and enables automatic feature retrieval at serving. `mlflow.sklearn.log_model()` does not capture this lineage.

questions.title: Question 5
questions.question: **`publish_online_features()` is called on a feature table. What does this do?**

choices.A: Creates a new version of the offline feature table
choices.B: Synchronizes the latest feature values from Delta (offline) to the online store
choices.C: Runs feature engineering computation jobs
choices.D: Registers the feature table in the UC registry

questions.shortAnswer: `publish_online_features()` pushes the latest feature values to the online (Redis-backed) store, making them available for real-time serving.

questions.title: Question 6
questions.question: **A model at serving time needs to retrieve user features using only the `user_id` from the request. What is required in the feature table?**

choices.A: The feature table must have `user_id` as the primary key and be published to the online store
choices.B: The feature table must be a managed Delta table
choices.C: The model must include the feature computation logic internally
choices.D: The feature table must be co-located in the same catalog as the model

questions.shortAnswer: For automatic feature retrieval at serving, the feature table needs `user_id` as primary key (for lookup) and values in the online store (for low-latency access).

questions.title: Question 7
questions.question: **How does `FeatureEngineeringClient.create_training_set()` prevent data leakage?**

choices.A: It filters out test set records
choices.B: It uses `timestamp_lookup_key` to join feature values that were available before each label's event timestamp
choices.C: It randomly shuffles the training data
choices.D: It drops features that are correlated with the target

questions.shortAnswer: The point-in-time join ensures features are joined only from the time period before the label's event occurred, preventing any future information from contaminating the training set.

questions.title: Question 8
questions.question: **A data scientist needs feature lineage for compliance but does not need real-time serving. Which approach is correct?**

choices.A: Use `fe.log_model()` with only the offline store configured (no online store required)
choices.B: Use `mlflow.sklearn.log_model()` with manual lineage documentation
choices.C: Skip feature lineage — it's only needed for real-time serving
choices.D: Use a regular Delta table instead of a feature table

questions.shortAnswer: `fe.log_model()` captures feature lineage (the connection between feature tables and the model) regardless of whether the online store is configured. The online store is only needed for real-time serving.

questions.title: Question 9
questions.question: **What happens when two rows with the same primary key are written to a feature table using `fe.write_table()`?**

choices.B: The existing row is updated (upserted) with the new values
choices.C: Both rows are retained (duplicates allowed)
choices.D: The new row is silently dropped

questions.shortAnswer: Feature tables are designed for upsert semantics — writing a row with an existing primary key updates the existing record.

questions.title: Question 10
questions.question: **A data scientist defines `space = {'n_estimators': hp.quniform('n_estimators', 100, 1000, 100)}`. What values can `n_estimators` take?**

choices.B: Integer multiples of 100 between 100 and 1000: 100, 200, ..., 1000
choices.C: Random floats between 0 and 1
choices.D: Only 100 and 1000

questions.shortAnswer: `hp.quniform(label, low, high, q)` returns values rounded to the nearest multiple of `q`. With q=100, values are 100, 200, 300, ..., 1000.

questions.title: Question 11
questions.question: **A Spark ML `CrossValidator` with `numFolds=3` and `parallelism=3` trains a grid of 9 configurations. How many models train simultaneously?**
questions.shortAnswer: `parallelism=3` means 3 models train simultaneously. Total models = 9 × 3 = 27, trained in batches of 3.

questions.title: Question 12
questions.question: **What is the main advantage of `hp.loguniform` over `hp.uniform` for a regularization parameter like `alpha` ranging from 0.0001 to 1.0?**

choices.B: `hp.loguniform` allocates equal probability to each order of magnitude (0.0001-0.001, 0.001-0.01, 0.01-0.1, 0.1-1.0), preventing bias toward large values
choices.C: `hp.loguniform` only works with positive values
choices.D: `hp.loguniform` is required for Bayesian optimization

questions.shortAnswer: On a linear scale, most trials would cluster near 1.0. Log scale ensures equal exploration across orders of magnitude, which matters for regularization and learning rate parameters.

questions.title: Question 13
questions.question: **AutoML is run on a classification task. Which outputs are produced?**

choices.B: A best-model notebook, a data exploration notebook, feature importance plots, and an MLflow experiment with all trial runs
choices.C: A hyperparameter configuration file only
choices.D: A trained model endpoint

questions.shortAnswer: Databricks AutoML produces: a best-trial notebook (editable), a data exploration notebook, an MLflow experiment with runs for all tested algorithms, and the best model registered.

questions.title: Question 14
questions.question: **The objective function for Hyperopt returns `1 - accuracy`. Why not return `-accuracy`?**

choices.B: Both are correct — either negation works because Hyperopt minimizes the returned loss
choices.C: `1 - accuracy` is more numerically stable
choices.D: `-accuracy` would cause Hyperopt to maximize, not minimize

questions.shortAnswer: Both `1 - accuracy` and `-accuracy` correctly signal to Hyperopt to maximize accuracy (by minimizing the returned loss). Both are valid; the convention varies by team.

questions.title: Question 15
questions.question: **A team runs Hyperopt with `SparkTrials(parallelism=4)` and `max_evals=40`. Bayesian optimization (TPE) requires results from previous trials to suggest the next configuration. How does this work with parallelism?**

choices.A: Parallelism is disabled when using TPE
choices.B: TPE runs 4 random trials first (no history), then uses available results to suggest batches of 4 subsequent trials
choices.C: Each parallel trial accesses the same history simultaneously
choices.D: Parallelism only works with random search, not TPE

questions.shortAnswer: With parallelism > 1, TPE initially makes semi-random suggestions for the first batch, then incorporates completed trial results to suggest the next batch. This reduces but does not eliminate the Bayesian efficiency advantage.

questions.title: Question 16
questions.question: **A model pipeline includes `VectorAssembler → StandardScaler → RandomForestClassifier`. A team wants to tune `RandomForestClassifier.numTrees` and `StandardScaler.withMean`. What object should wrap the tuning?**

choices.B: Wrap the entire `Pipeline` in `CrossValidator` — `ParamGridBuilder` can reference any stage's params
choices.C: Separate `CrossValidator` per stage
choices.D: Use Hyperopt on each stage independently

questions.shortAnswer: `CrossValidator` wraps the full Pipeline. `ParamGridBuilder` uses `stage.paramName` syntax to reference params across any stage.

questions.title: Question 17
questions.question: **What does `no_progress_loss(n)` do in a Hyperopt early stopping function?**

choices.A: Stops if loss increases for n consecutive trials
choices.B: Stops if no improvement is seen in the best loss across the last n trials
choices.C: Stops after n total trials regardless of improvement
choices.D: Stops if any trial takes longer than n seconds

questions.shortAnswer: `no_progress_loss(n)` is a built-in early stopping function that halts the search if the best observed loss hasn't improved in the last n trials.

questions.title: Question 18
questions.question: **A data scientist wants to compare a fixed set of 8 hyperparameter combinations exhaustively. Which search strategy is most appropriate?**

choices.B: Random search with max_evals=8
choices.C: Grid search — exhaustive when search space is fully enumerable and small
choices.D: AutoML

questions.shortAnswer: When the search space is small and fully known (8 combinations), grid search guarantees every combination is evaluated. Bayesian optimization overhead is unnecessary for such small spaces.

questions.title: Question 19
questions.question: **A model version is in the UC registry. What is the correct way to load the latest version tagged with alias `champion`?**

choices.B: `mlflow.pyfunc.load_model("models:/catalog.schema.model@champion")`
choices.C: `MlflowClient().get_model_version_by_alias(name, "champion")`
choices.D: `mlflow.pyfunc.load_model(f"models:/catalog.schema.model/{version_number}")`

questions.shortAnswer: `mlflow.pyfunc.load_model("models:/catalog.schema.model@champion")` loads the version currently pointed to by the `champion` alias.

questions.title: Question 20
questions.question: **A team registers a model with `mlflow.sklearn.log_model(model, "model", registered_model_name="fraud_clf")`. Which registry does this go to if `set_registry_uri` was not called?**

choices.B: Workspace model registry
choices.C: The call fails — must call `set_registry_uri` first
choices.D: A temporary registry that expires after 30 days

questions.shortAnswer: Without `mlflow.set_registry_uri("databricks-uc")`, models are registered to the workspace registry (not UC). UC features like GRANT and lineage are unavailable.

questions.title: Question 21
questions.question: **A `ServedModelInput` is configured with `workload_size=SMALL` and a traffic of 100 RPS arrives. What happens?**

choices.A: Requests are queued until compute scales up
choices.B: The endpoint auto-scales to Medium/Large compute to handle the load
choices.C: Requests fail with 429 Too Many Requests until manually scaled
choices.D: SMALL size is fixed — it cannot auto-scale

questions.shortAnswer: Databricks Model Serving queues requests and auto-scales the serving compute based on traffic, with some queuing latency during scale-up.

questions.title: Question 22
questions.question: **Model signatures with `infer_signature()` are inferred from training data. Why might this cause a serving error?**

choices.A: Signatures are only valid for 30 days
choices.B: If the training data has extra columns (e.g., label column), the signature includes them; serving requests without those columns will fail schema validation
choices.C: `infer_signature` doesn't work for sklearn models
choices.D: Signatures with more than 10 features cause serving errors

questions.shortAnswer: Always infer signature from the feature input (X) only, not the full training DataFrame including the label column.

questions.title: Question 23
questions.question: **In a multi-task Databricks Job, Task B `depends_on` Task A with `run_if: ALL_SUCCESS`. Task A is skipped because its condition was false. What happens to Task B?**

choices.B: Task B is also skipped
choices.C: Task B fails
choices.D: Task B waits indefinitely

questions.shortAnswer: If a dependency is skipped, downstream tasks with `ALL_SUCCESS` are also skipped (not failed), since the condition was "skipped" rather than "failed".

questions.title: Question 24
questions.question: **A team wants to compare champion vs challenger performance using inference tables. Which SQL query approach identifies predictions from each model?**

choices.B: Extract `served_model_name` from the request JSON field
choices.C: Filter by `model_version` column
choices.D: Join with the MLflow experiment table

questions.shortAnswer: The inference table's `request` JSON contains the `served_model_name` field, which identifies which endpoint configuration (champion or challenger) served each request.

questions.title: Question 25
questions.question: **What is a "blue-green deployment" in the context of ML model serving?**

choices.A: Splitting traffic 50/50 between two model versions
choices.B: Running two full serving environments (blue=current production, green=new version), then switching all traffic from blue to green atomically
choices.C: Gradually increasing challenger traffic from 10% to 100%
choices.D: Running shadow deployment alongside champion

questions.shortAnswer: Blue-green switches the entire traffic to the new version in one step after the new environment is validated, providing instant rollback capability by switching back to blue.

questions.title: Question 26
questions.question: **A model registered with `fe.log_model()` is deployed. A request comes in with only `{"user_id": 12345}`. How does the endpoint respond?**

choices.A: Error — insufficient features in the request
choices.B: The endpoint looks up all required features from the online store using `user_id` and returns a prediction
choices.C: A prediction of null is returned
choices.D: The endpoint requests the missing features from the client

questions.shortAnswer: This is the key Feature Engineering in UC serving pattern: the model knows which features to retrieve and automatically looks them up from the online store using the entity key.

questions.title: Question 27
questions.question: **`mlflow.evaluate()` is called after training. What does it return?**

choices.B: An `EvaluationResult` with metrics, artifacts, and a logged MLflow run
choices.C: A report in PDF format
choices.D: A serving endpoint configuration

questions.shortAnswer: `mlflow.evaluate()` returns an `EvaluationResult` object containing computed metrics, generated artifacts (confusion matrix, etc.), and logs everything to MLflow.

questions.title: Question 28
questions.question: **A Python wheel task is preferred over a notebook task in a Databricks Job because:**

choices.B: Python wheel tasks are versioned, testable, and installable as library packages — better for production pipelines
choices.C: Notebook tasks cannot use cluster libraries
choices.D: Python wheels use less cluster memory

questions.shortAnswer: Python wheel tasks encapsulate code as testable, versioned packages, making them more suitable for production ML pipelines than ad-hoc notebooks.

questions.title: Question 29
questions.question: **What should happen to the `previous_champion` alias after a successful canary completion and full traffic migration?**

choices.B: Delete it once the new champion has been stable in production for your team's defined stability period (e.g., 2 weeks)
choices.C: Rename it to `archived`
choices.D: It is automatically deleted by Databricks after 30 days

questions.shortAnswer: Keep `previous_champion` as a rapid rollback option initially. Once the new champion is stable, clean up stale aliases to avoid confusion. The retention period is team-defined.

questions.title: Question 30
questions.question: **Which configuration option on a serving endpoint helps ensure consistent low latency for a high-SLA fraud detection service?**

choices.B: `scale_to_zero_enabled=False` with appropriate `workload_size`
choices.C: `max_concurrent_requests=1`
choices.D: `traffic_percentage=100` for the champion

questions.shortAnswer: Keeping compute always-on (scale_to_zero disabled) eliminates cold start latency. Appropriate workload size ensures enough capacity for peak traffic.

questions.title: Question 31
questions.question: **A serving endpoint receives 1,000 requests per second. 5 requests returned status 500. What is the error rate?**

choices.C: 0.005% (5 out of 100,000 if measured over 100 seconds)
choices.D: Cannot calculate without knowing the time window

questions.shortAnswer: 5 errors / 1000 requests = 0.5% error rate (for that specific second; requires time windowing for a meaningful rate metric).

questions.title: Question 32
questions.question: **A team needs to implement a promotion gate that compares challenger AUC to champion AUC. The champion alias must be updated only if the challenger improves by ≥ 0.5%. Which tool runs this logic?**

choices.B: A notebook task in a Databricks Job, using `MlflowClient` to compare metrics and `set_registered_model_alias` to promote
choices.C: MLflow autolog automatic promotion
choices.D: Lakehouse Monitoring auto-promotion

questions.shortAnswer: Promotion gate logic is custom Python code in a Databricks Job task that: loads both models, evaluates on holdout, compares metrics, and promotes if the threshold is met.

questions.title: Question 33
questions.question: **A team's model has PSI = 0.22 on the `income_bracket` feature. What is the recommended first step?**

choices.A: Immediately retrain with all available data
choices.B: Investigate why `income_bracket` has shifted — check upstream data pipeline and data quality
choices.C: Roll back to the previous model version
choices.D: Ignore — PSI above 0.2 is normal

questions.shortAnswer: PSI > 0.2 indicates significant drift, but the root cause must be determined first. If it's an upstream data pipeline issue (wrong data, schema change), retraining on bad data would make things worse.

questions.title: Question 34
questions.question: **Which LHM output table stores per-window statistical profiles (mean, stddev, quantiles) of each feature?**

choices.B: `_monitor_info`
choices.C: `_profile_metrics`
choices.D: `_alert_history`

questions.shortAnswer: The `_profile_metrics` table stores statistical profiles (mean, std, min, max, quantiles, null counts) per feature per time window.

questions.title: Question 35
questions.question: **A compliance officer asks: "Who changed the `champion` alias for `ml_catalog.fraud_models.fraud_classifier` on Jan 15?" Where is this information found?**

choices.B: `system.access.audit` filtered by `actionName = 'updateModelVersionAlias'` and date
choices.C: Databricks cluster logs
choices.D: The model's `description` field

questions.shortAnswer: UC audit logs in `system.access.audit` record all alias update events with user identity, timestamp, and the specific model name.

questions.title: Question 36
questions.question: **A team runs bias testing before deployment and finds disparate impact = 0.73. What should happen next?**

choices.A: Deploy the model — 0.73 is close enough to 0.80
choices.B: Block deployment — investigate and remediate the bias before promoting to production
choices.C: Deploy with a disclaimer in the model card
choices.D: Retrain with twice the minority group data

questions.shortAnswer: Disparate impact of 0.73 is below the four-fifths rule threshold of 0.80. Deployment should be blocked until the bias is investigated and remediated to meet the fairness threshold.

questions.title: Question 37
questions.question: **An MLflow run logs `mlflow.log_input(dataset, context="training")`. What does this enable?**

choices.A: Automatic retraining when the dataset changes
choices.B: UC data lineage: the Delta table version used for training is linked to the model version in the lineage graph
choices.C: Dataset versioning in the MLflow artifact store
choices.D: Automatic data quality monitoring

questions.shortAnswer: `log_input` with a `DeltaDatasetSource` creates a UC lineage link between the specific Delta table version and the model version, enabling auditors to trace exactly which data trained which model.

questions.title: Question 38
questions.question: **Lakehouse Monitoring is configured with `granularities=["1 day", "1 week"]`. What does this mean?**

choices.A: Monitoring runs once per day and once per week
choices.B: Drift and profile metrics are computed at both daily and weekly time windows
choices.C: Alerts fire every day and every week
choices.D: Data is retained for 1 day or 1 week depending on the monitor type

questions.shortAnswer: Granularities specify the time windows over which metrics are computed. `["1 day", "1 week"]` produces both daily and weekly aggregated metrics in the output tables.

questions.title: Question 39
questions.question: **Model cards provide which compliance benefit?**

choices.B: Document intended use, limitations, evaluation metrics, and fairness considerations — required for regulatory model risk management
choices.C: Automatically generate audit reports
choices.D: Replace manual code review

questions.shortAnswer: Model cards (stored as MLflow registered model descriptions and version tags) provide structured documentation about the model's purpose, performance, and risks — essential for SR 11-7 and similar frameworks.

questions.title: Question 40
questions.question: **After a model is promoted to production, the baseline table for LHM should be updated. When is the right time?**

choices.A: Immediately when any new data arrives
choices.B: After the new model has been stable in production for a defined period (e.g., 1-2 weeks), and updated to reflect the new model's distribution
choices.C: Never — the original baseline should always be the training data
choices.D: Daily, automatically

questions.shortAnswer: Update the baseline after the new model is confirmed stable. Updating too early may mask genuine drift; updating too late means drift is compared against the wrong reference distribution.

questions.title: Question 41
questions.question: **A model uses PII (social security numbers) as a training feature. GDPR requires the right to erasure for a specific user. What is the correct approach?**

choices.A: Delete the model — it was trained on PII
choices.B: Document that the training data included the user's PII but the model artifact itself doesn't store individual records; apply Delta column masking for future feature access and document the data source in the model card
choices.C: Retrain the model without the user's data
choices.D: Encrypt the user's records in the feature table

questions.shortAnswer: Standard ML practice: document which data was used, apply masking to prevent future access to deleted records, and retain the model artifact (which is an aggregation, not a record). Retraining is optional and resource-intensive.

questions.title: Question 42
questions.question: **Which drift type can be detected WITHOUT ground truth labels?**

choices.B: Performance drift
choices.C: Data drift and prediction drift
choices.D: Label drift

questions.shortAnswer: Data drift (input distribution comparison) and prediction drift (output distribution comparison) require only historical feature/prediction distributions. Concept drift requires labels to compute performance metrics.

questions.title: Question 43
questions.question: **A governance team requires that every production ML model be explainable. Which MLflow feature enables storing explainability artifacts with each model version?**

choices.B: `mlflow.log_artifact("shap_summary.png")` within the training run, linked to the registered model version via the run ID
choices.C: The MLflow Model Registry automatically generates SHAP plots
choices.D: `mlflow.pyfunc.log_model(explainability=True)`

questions.shortAnswer: SHAP artifacts (summary plots, values CSV) are logged as run artifacts. Since model versions link to their training run, auditors can access explainability artifacts via the run ID stored in the model version metadata.

questions.title: Question 44
questions.question: **A UC model object has the alias `champion` pointing to version 5. A new `champion` alias is set to version 6. What happens to version 5?**

choices.B: Version 5 remains in the registry but no longer has the `champion` alias — it becomes an "unaliased" version
choices.C: Version 5's `champion` alias is renamed to `previous_champion` automatically
choices.D: Version 5 is archived (moved to Archived stage)

questions.shortAnswer: Setting an alias to a new version simply moves the pointer. The old version remains in the registry with all its other tags/metadata, just without that alias.

questions.title: Question 45
questions.question: **A serving endpoint's p99 latency has been increasing by 15ms per week for 4 weeks. AUC is stable. What is the most likely cause?**

choices.B: Data drift
choices.C: Operational degradation — possibly growing request payload size, feature store latency, or endpoint compute fragmentation
choices.D: Model version mismatch

questions.shortAnswer: Stable AUC with increasing latency indicates a non-model-quality issue: operational factors like growing feature retrieval time, larger request payloads, or serving compute degradation are most likely.
